Trying to Wrap My Head Around the JavaScript Prototype Chain

I went back and forth on whether to write this one. I was honestly scared that if I recorded something wrong here, I'd end up dragging other people down with me. But then I remembered why I started this Hexo blog in the first place — it's basically my own notebook — and that made me feel a bit better about it. So here's a note on the JavaScript prototype chain.
Before we get into the prototype chain, there's one thing you have to understand first: JavaScript doesn't actually have classes. The
you see is just a piece of syntactic sugar that ES6 added.
(Syntactic sugar = a little sweetener that makes things easier to write, nothing more.)

So what's the idea behind the prototype chain? Let me try an analogy using frontend tech. When we learn frontend, most of us follow a path like this: HTML & CSS > JavaScript > a JavaScript library or framework.

Think of a junior frontend dev who only knows HTML & CSS and JavaScript. Those three are the foundation of everything in frontend. Everything you learn afterwards — jade/pug, Sass/SCSS, jQuery, Vue.js, Angular, React, and so on — is built on top of those three. That's why, when you're using Vue.js, you can still write plain JavaScript. And along the way, if you use something that isn't part of Vue.js's own API, it'll look up the chain to see whether JavaScript itself supports that syntax (Vue.js > JavaScript).
(That comparison was something I came up with on the spot, so bear with me if it's rough.)
If that doesn't quite land, here's another take on the prototype chain from my friend Yu-Hsuan:

If I had to explain it to someone,
I'd compare "prototype" to a game.

Take Ragnarok Online for example.
The Novice class is like the prototype of every other class.
No matter what you become later — first job, second job,
third job, transcendent second job —

you inherit everything from the Novice.

Which means every Novice skill (method) is available to all of you.

So if you're a Rune Knight, you can still use First Aid
(a method inherited from the Novice — the Rune Knight itself
doesn't have it, so when a Rune Knight uses it, the lookup goes
Rune Knight > Lord Knight > Knight > Swordman > Novice),
walking back up the chain looking for the First Aid skill (method).
Both of those explanations cover the prototype chain and inheritance.

Let's walk through it with some diagrams.
First we declare a variable and turn it into a prototype.
Then we add stuff onto the prototype.
Because of that, the path our first
is a little different though. The original object doesn't have

And of course, when we hit

The core idea here is exactly the same as above — only the syntax differs, and it's more intuitive. Think about it: if every piece of data had to hang off the prototype, you'd end up with a ton of
. That's not intuitive, and it makes the code messy.

The nice thing about the sugar is that everything's managed in one place. Otherwise you'd have
scattered all over, which is genuinely painful to manage — hard to find, and you have to read the whole file just to figure out which prototype a thing belongs to.

I probably still haven't explained prototypes all that clearly. I should dig into this more down the road, because it's a kind of peculiar topic — I've read a bunch of articles on it and people come at it from a lot of different angles.
